does: This paper develops a mechanistic interpretability framework for understanding and predicting tool-use failures in AI agents before they occur. The core contribution combines Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) and linear probes to decompose model activations into interpretable features, enabling early detection of whether a tool will be invoked and prediction of associated risks. The framework operates by reading model internal states before action execution, identifying which layers and features drive tool-use decisions. By providing real-time internal signals rather than relying on post-hoc external observation, it enables proactive diagnosis of failure modes like skipped necessary tool calls, unnecessary invocations, or risky actions in long-horizon agentic workflows.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of mechanistic interpretability research, particularly advances in Sparse Autoencoders as a method for discovering interpretable features in neural networks. It addresses a practical gap identified in recent work on AI agent safety and reliability—that existing observability methods (prompt analysis, output evaluation, execution logs) are fundamentally reactive and insufficient for high-stakes deployments. The paper extends prior work on probing-based interpretability to the specific challenge of agent tool use, connecting to broader research on controlling model behavior, understanding decision-making in transformer agents, and improving the trustworthiness of autonomous systems in enterprise settings.
enables: This framework opens multiple directions for safer agentic AI deployment. It could support real-time intervention mechanisms that flag or prevent risky tool calls before execution, reducing costly downstream errors in long-horizon tasks. The mechanistic insights into tool-use representations may enable better training procedures that guide models toward more reliable decision-making. The approach generalizes beyond tool use to other critical decision points in agentic workflows, potentially creating a foundation for broader internal safety monitoring. It also enables research into fine-tuning or architectural modifications that strengthen the internal signals associated with appropriate tool use, and supports empirical study of how tool-use reasoning emerges during model scaling and training.
